text: The Li-S battery chemistry has high theoretical energy density, but it has a variety of kinetic issues. Over the past several years, many different catalyst materials have been explored for Li-S batteries and the concept of catalysis is widely accepted. However, the Li-S battery does not fit into the traditional catalysis model, which involves a reduction of the activation energy barrier of the rate-determining step (RDS) in the reaction mechanism, and the catalyst has no effect on the products. Thus, whether there is a RDS in Li-S batteries, and if so, whether and how it can be catalyzed, and how a catalyst can continue to act on the reaction surface after the surface is covered with sulfur products are all unknowns. Here we report for the first time the RDS of Li-S batteries under lean electrolyte conditions and the catalysis of it that fits into the traditional catalysis concept. We carefully studied the electrochemistry of Li-S batteries under lean electrolyte conditions, and proposed an updated mechanism of the Li-S battery: S8 solid was first reduced to Li2S8 and Li2S4 molecular species sequentially; the reduction of Li2S4 to a Li2S2-Li2S solid with a constant ratio of 1:4 was the RDS in the discharge process, and the Li2S2-to-Li2S solid-solid conversion proposed by others did not occur; and the charging process was fast. Catalysts of supported cobalt phthalocyanine molecules were found to catalyze the potential-determining step (PDS). Even though the Li2S2/Li2S produced at the end of the discharge reaction had covered the active sites, it could still self-catalyze the reaction and drove the continuous discharge process.
